unitTitle: KS2 Y4 Desktop publishing (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Words and pictures
lessons.objectives:
I can explain the difference between text and images
I can say how text and images send a message clearly
I can name good and bad points of text and images
I can use emojis kindly online
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: text · images · communicate · advantages · disadvantages · emoji. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; the signs picture on the board. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — what does this sign mean? Pupils look at the signs grid, CHOOSE whether a sign uses text or an image, then SORT signs into ‘just a picture’ vs ‘words and a picture’.
   • Support: choose which sign is a picture only.  • Core: one good thing about a picture.  • Challenge: when do words help a picture.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — text vs image. Teacher: TEXT = words you read; IMAGE = a picture. Use the customs (Douane/Zoll) example: text does not help if you cannot read the language. Likely error: ‘a picture is always best’ → fix-words “match the sign to the people who read it.”
3. (20–35) Good and bad points. Pupils SORT facts into good/not-so-good about images on the activity worksheet, then a multi-select on using emojis kindly.
   • Support: choose one good point of text/image.  • Core: one good + one bad point of text.  • Challenge: pick text/image/both for a multi-language sign and justify.
4. (35–50) You-do — make a message that uses BOTH a word and a picture; photograph it onto the activity worksheet. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; each pupil shows one message that uses a word and a picture.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks ‘less done’. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: Signs from the world around us (Teach Computing).png

lessons.title: Can you edit it?
lessons.objectives:
I can change font style, size and colour for a purpose
I can edit text using the keyboard keys
I can explain that changing text makes a message clearer
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: font · style · size · colour · edit · template. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; the invitation template open in the desktop publishing app. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — which tool does which job? Pupils SORT tools into change-colour / change-size / change-style, then LABEL the parts of the party invitation (title, picture, date).
   • Support: choose what makes text bigger / blue.  • Core: one change to make the invitation clearer.  • Challenge: two changes to make the title stand out.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — what is desktop publishing? Making documents with text and images using page layout software. Show the three font changes: SIZE (how big), COLOUR, STYLE (the shape of letters). Likely error: mixing size and style → fix-words “size = how BIG, style = the SHAPE of the letters.”
3. (20–30) The keyboard keys. Shift = capital, Backspace = delete the letter before, Return = new line. Pupils fill the gaps on the activity worksheet.
4. (30–50) You-do — edit the invitation: change font size, colour and style and move the text; screenshot it onto the activity worksheet. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil shows one change they made.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Great template!
lessons.objectives:
I can explain what page orientation means
I can recognise placeholders and say why they help
I can create a template for a magazine cover
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: orientation · portrait · landscape · template · placeholder · layout. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; the desktop publishing app ready with a blank document. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — which way round is the page? Pupils CHOOSE portrait vs landscape, SORT documents into portrait/landscape, then LABEL the placeholders on a template (title / image / text).
   • Support: choose the magazine’s orientation.  • Core: what a placeholder does.  • Challenge: why a template beats a blank page.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — a template is a ready-made layout; placeholders are empty boxes that show where things go. Show the example magazine cover (title at top, main picture, smaller text). Likely error: typing real content now → fix-words “placeholders first, real content next lesson.”
3. (20–25) Orientation. PORTRAIT = tall (magazine), LANDSCAPE = wide (postcard). Hold up A4 both ways.
4. (25–50) You-do — make a magazine-cover template with placeholders for the title, a picture and some text (no real content yet); screenshot it onto the activity worksheet. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil points to a placeholder on their template.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Becoming a designer
lessons.objectives:
I can find and open my saved template
I can paste text and pictures to make a magazine cover
I can make changes to my content after I add it
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: content · copy · paste · editorial designer. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; each pupil’s template from last lesson is findable in the app. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — the editorial designer (the person who decides where text and pictures go). Pupils CHOOSE what the designer does, then ORDER the copy-and-paste steps.
   • Support: choose paste / why the template helps.  • Core: why copy-paste beats retyping.  • Challenge: how to move text to the right placeholder.
2. (10–15) Find your work. Open the app and find last lesson’s template in recent designs. Reassure anyone who cannot find theirs; TA helps locate it.
3. (15–25) I-do → we-do — copy and paste text into a placeholder; add a picture (search, click, drag, resize). Likely error: pasting into the wrong box → fix-words “click the right box first, then paste.”
4. (25–50) You-do — add your text and a picture to your cover, then change them; ORDER the add-a-picture steps, multi-select what makes a good cover, and screenshot the finished cover onto the activity worksheet. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.” Remind pupils of the school rule for searching for images.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil shows one change they made after adding content.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Lay it out
lessons.objectives:
I can name different layouts
I can match a layout to its purpose
I can choose a suitable layout for a message
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: layout · purpose. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; the layout pictures on the board. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — name the layout. Pupils look at the newspaper and poster pictures, CHOOSE the layout from a description, then SORT clues onto poster / newspaper / postcard.
   • Support: best layout for a far-away quick message.  • Core: name a layout and its purpose.  • Challenge: why a poster and a newspaper look so different.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — a layout is how text and pictures are arranged; the layout depends on the PURPOSE. Newspaper = detailed news in columns; poster = quick eye-catching message.
3. (20–35) Match the layout to its purpose (matching task on the activity worksheet): poster / newspaper / postcard.
   • Support: choose the best layout for a scenario.  • Core: which layout for ‘the play is tomorrow’.  • Challenge: a layout for the head teacher’s whole-area news.
4. (35–50) You-do — choose a layout for one scenario and say why; screenshot or photograph the choice onto the activity worksheet. Likely error: choosing by ‘what looks nice’ not purpose → fix-words “match the layout to the job.” TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil names one layout and its purpose.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Why desktop publishing?
lessons.objectives:
I can give real-world uses of desktop publishing
I can say why desktop publishing is helpful
I can compare desktop publishing with work made by hand
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: desktop publishing · benefit · real world. TA: everyone has the starter worksheet; A4 paper and pens ready for the by-hand poster. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–10) Starter — what is desktop publishing? Pupils fill the gaps (text and images using page layout software), then multi-select who uses it in the real world (estate agent, shop, magazine editor).
   • Support: choose what DTP makes.  • Core: a job that uses DTP and what they make.  • Challenge: explain DTP in your own words.
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — real-world uses (newspapers, estate-agent leaflets, shop posters) and the benefits: FAST, EASY TO CHANGE, NEAT.
3. (20–40) The race — pupils make a poster by hand while the teacher makes one in the app; then SORT facts into ‘good thing about desktop publishing’ vs ‘hard about doing it by hand’ on the activity worksheet. Use the ‘2pm→3pm’ change and ‘20 copies’ to show why DTP wins. Likely error: thinking by hand is just as quick → fix-words “one change, one click — not a whole redraw.”
   • Support: which is quicker for 20 copies / a time change.  • Core: one benefit of DTP.  • Challenge: two reasons a shopkeeper would switch to DTP.
4. (40–50) You-do — look back at your own DTP work; write one thing it helped you do and screenshot it onto the activity worksheet. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; recap the unit journey: words & pictures → editing → templates & placeholders → adding content → layouts → why it helps.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
